Title: China Development Bank provides $356 million USD for Phase C of the 350 MW Candiota Thermoelectric power plant
Description: In November 2004, China Development (CDB), Eletrobras CGTEE S.A., and CITIC Group Corporation Ltd. signed a framework agreement for Phase C of the 350 MW Candiota power plant (or the Presidente Médici Candiota Power Station Project) (Chinese agreement name: 《巴西坎迪奥塔火电厂二期EPC项目合作框架协议》) (see '中国电建集团深拓巴西市场纪实'). The agreement was signed in the presence of President of Brazil Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva and Chinese President Hu Jintao. At the time, the estimated investment into the project was $256 million USD, with CDB financing 90% and CGTEE covering the remaining 10% (see 'Chineses chegam e dÃ£o continuidade Ã s negociaÃ§Ãµes de construÃ§Ã£o da Fase C de Candiota'). On August 3, 2006, the Federal Senate of Brazil authorized CGTEE to take on this $430 million USD of external debt, and the loan agreement should be signed within 270 days of this authorization (see 'RESOLUÇÃO Nº 34, DE 2006').On April 20, 2007, CDB, BNP Paribas S.A., and CGTEE signed a $430 million USD financing agreement for the plant (see 'Candiota'). It was signed through two contracts, an export credit contract, of $281 million USD, and one commercial contract, of $149 million USD. The remaining funding was contributed by CGTEE (see 'ACÓRDÃO 1996/2008 - PLENÁRIO'). Of this amount, CDB contributed $356 million USD (see 'BU_PITCH_ENGLISH_2019'). CITIC Construction reported the loan to be a buyer's credit loan. The interest and the principal payments of this debt were to be made semiannually in June and December. The funds from the financing agreement were fully drawn upon by May 2010. The outstanding balance as of December 31, 2014 was R$682.4 million BRL (see pg.19 of 'Eletrobras Management Proposal - Restatement'). According to the 2007 Eletrobras Annual Report, the 281 million USD contract, which would be used for the acquisition of equipment and services, had an interest rate of LIBOR + 0.75% and a maturity of 15 years, and the 149 million USD contract, which would be used for other project expenses, would have an interest rate of LIBOR + 1.30% and a maturity of 8 years. The average 6-month LIBOR in April 2007 was 5.354%. Each tranche would have a grant element below 25% based on these loan terms, so this loan has been coded as non-concessional.China Export & Credit Insurance Corporation (Sinosure) originally provided buyer's credit insurance for this debt. However, it was replaced as an insurer by CDB itself on May 8, 2008, at the demand of the Chinese Government. Sinosure's replacement was related to issues regarding the project's contracts and financing, which caused considerable delays (see 'ACÓRDÃO 1996/2008 - PLENÁRIO').This project sought to construct and install a 350 MW subcritical condensing coal-fired unit in the Candiota plant (see '中国电建集团深拓巴西市场纪实'). The Candiota plant was located in the Municipality of Candiota, located in the southern half of the state of Rio Grande do Sul, about 400 kilometers from Porto Alegre (see 'ACÓRDÃO 1996/2008 - PLENÁRIO'). In addition to CITIC, PowerChina SEPCO1 Electric Power Construction Co., Ltd. (SEPCO1) was a subcontractor responsible for project implementation. It signed an agreement with CITC to implement the project on August 19, 2006; it was responsible for the engineering design, supply of materials and equipment, construction (under an engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) contract), installation, commissioning, testing, and trial operation and repair. The project's groundbreaking ceremony was held on September 25, 2006. The project was completed on December 29, 2010, after a successful 168-hour long full-load trial run (see '中国电建集团深拓巴西市场纪实').The Candiota plant’s Units A and B, which were in operation prior to SEPCO1's implementation the third phase of the project, have experienced serious issues. In 2016, the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA) closed the plant because of its failure to meet pollutant emission control parameters; IBAMA suspended at Units A and B and fined the plant $23 million USD upon discovering violations in the maximum flow of effluents and the rates of grease and oil (see 'Chinese involvement in Brazilian coal power sector causes concern').

Title: CDB provides $750 million loan to BNDES for construction of Third Section of the GASENE Pipeline
Description: On December 27, 2007, China Development Bank (CDB) provided a $750 million loan to the Brazil Development Bank (BNDES), which it then on-lent to Transportadora Gasene (a subsidiary of Petrobras, the Brazilian national petroleum company). The loan is fully disbursed. However, its borrowing terms are unknown. Transportadora Gasene was responsible for the construction and future ownership of pipelines for transport of natural gas with a total length of 1.4 thousand kilometers and a transport capacity of 20 million cubic meter per day, connecting the Cabiúnas Terminal in Rio De Janeiro to the city of Catu, in the state of Bahia. The Gasene project sought to establish a natural gas pipeline between northeast and southeast Brazil. The CDB loan was to be used to fund the Cacimbas-Catu phase of the project (also known as GASCAC or GASCAV). Previously, in 2004, BNDES and Transportada Gasene signed an agreement for the construction of the $1.3 billion GASENE Southeast-Northeast Integration Pipeline in Brazil. The project was broken into three sections: Cabiunas-Vitoria, due for completion in 2008; Vitoria-Cacimbas, which began operating in November 2007; and the Cacimbas-Catu pipeline, funded by CDB, a 946 km segment whose construction began in the first quarter of 2008 and ended in March 2010. The pipeline was then commissioned and put into use in September 2010. Sinopec was responsible for the construction of the Cabiunas-Vitoria and Cacimbas-Catu sections of the pipeline. The remaining costs were funded by BNDES. China Eximbank was originally set to fund the project; however, a contract could not be reached with BNDES over the percent of local labor to be used.

Title: [Drawn down amount] China Development Bank provides $10 billion line of credit for oil exploration in the Santos Basin (Linked to Project ID#36737)
Description: On November 3, 2009, the China Development Bank (CDB) issued a $10 billion line of credit to Petroleo Brasileiro S.A.(Petrobras) for the exploration of the Santos Basin pre-salt oil fields (captured in Project ID#36737).  All borrowings under the line of credit carried the following terms: a 10-year maturity and an annual interest rate of LIBOR + a 2.8% margin (6-month USD LIBOR + 2.8 = 0.517+2.8=3.317%). As a source of collateral, Petrobras signed a ten-year oil-supply contract with Sinopec’s (subsidiary) trading company, UNIPEC Asia, and agreed to provide 150,000 barrels per day in the first year (2009) and 200,000 barrels per day for the following nine years (2010-2019). Sinopec agreed to pay market prices for Petrobras’ oil and deposit payments for the oil in Petrobras’ CDB account, and the borrower (Petrobras) agreed to maintain a minimum account balance equivalent to six months of interest payments. As of December 31, 2009, $3 billion of the $10 billion credit line had been utilized (i.e. drawn down) by the borrower. Then, on February 10, 2010, Petrobras withdrew an additional $2 billion from the credit line.  The borrower ultimately drew down $7 billion in total from the credit line (as captured via Project ID#38420). It was originally envisaged that $3 billion from the credit line would be utilized to purchase Chinese equipment. However, Petrobras ultimately decided not to do so. The loan proceeds were to be used by the borrower for oil exploration activities in the Santos Basin, which was part of a larger effort to upgrade the refinery capabilities of Petrobras.  Sinopec — a Chinese state-owned oil company — and Petrobras were jointly responsible for implementing the project. In January 2018, Petrobras pre-paid the residual balance of the oil supply contract, effectively ending its oil sales commitment to Sinopec.

Title: CDB Financing in Syndicated Loan to Brazil for Refinancing of Black Gold Two Semi-Submersible Drilling Platforms
Description: In December 2009, a $800 million syndicated loan to refinance Black Gold Two Semi-Submersible Drilling Platforms was signed. The debt component includes CDB financing. The amount of CDB financing and the loan terms are unclear. Delays in construction of two semi submersible drilling platforms have forced Black Gold Drilling to seek to extend its debt tenor by one year. Tranche A and B of the project will both be extended by 1.25 years. It is unclear if CDB was involved in the original round of debt financing, flow type coded as loan for now. The agreement amends and restructures an existing Credit Agreement dated 25 October 2007. The rigs were built by Singapore’s Yantai Raffles Shipyard Company at its facilities in China. They will be operated under five and seven-year contracts with Petrobras. The rigs are designed to operate at depths of up to 2,000m and 2,400m respectively - with a drilling depth of 7,500m below the seabed. Petrobras will likely use them in the Campos Basin offshore Brazil.

Title: China Exim Bank loans $240,000,000 to State Grid Brazil Holding S.A.
Description: In 2010, the China Exim Bank agreed to loan $240,000,000 to State Grid Brazil Holding S.A.  The loan has an interest rate of six-month Libor plus 2% per year (2010 6 month LIBOR average + 2 = 2.519%). Interest is to be amortized quarterly, beginning March 2011 and the total amount of the loan must be paid in 60 months.

Title: China Development Bank Refinances $96 Million to pay down existing debt associated with the 2007 Sevan Driller deal
Description: On March 25, 2011, the China Development Bank provided $96 million in debt financing to pay down existing debt associated with the 2007 Sevan Driller deal, and to cover general costs. This is part of a larger $480 million syndicated loan to refinance the project. The facility will be used to repay the existing 1st and 2nd lien financing on the Sevan Driller and for general corporate purposes. The original Sevan Driller deal was signed in 2007. Other participants in the syndicated loan include GIEK, ING Group, DVB Bank, NIBC Bank.

Title: China Development Bank disburses $380 million of Credit Facility to Telemar for refinancing (linked to #59418)
Description: In July 2011, the China Development Bank disbursed $380 million to Telemar (a Brazilian communications company now known as Oi) of the $500 million credit facility to refinance Telemar's debt. In June 2011, Telemar entered into a stand-by credit facility with China Development Bank under which China Development Bank for US $500 million (#59418). The proceeds of this stand-by credit facility have been and will be used to refinance other indebtedness of Telemar. Loans under this stand-by credit facility bear interest at the rate of LIBOR plus 2.30% per annum (6 Month USD Libor Avg. of June 2011 + 2.5= 2.898%), payable semi-annually through maturity in June 2016. The principal of these loans is payable commencing in October 2014. At December 31, 2012, the outstanding principal amount under this credit facility was US$380 million.

Title: CDB signs $250 million non Sinosure-covered credit agreement with Nextel Brazil for construction of 3G network (Linked to Project ID#59821)
Description: On April 20, 2012, China Development Bank signed an agreement for a $500 million credit facility to Nextel Telecomunicações Ltda. (“Nextel Brazil”), the Brazilian operating subsidiary of NII Holdings, Inc., to finance the cost of network infrastructure equipment and the deployment of the WCDMA (3G phone) network in Brazil, a collaboration between Huawei and Nextel. According to a 2019 SEC document, the 500 million credit facility was divided between a 250 million Sinosure Credit Agreement and a 250 million non-Sinosure Credit agreement. This project records the 250 million non-Sinosure Credit Agreement. The Sinosure Credit Agreement is captured in #59821. The financing of the agreement has a floating interest rate set to LIBOR plus 2.90% (3.15% and 3.21% as of December 31, 2013 and 2012, respectively). The loans made under this agreement have a  three-year borrowing period, a seven-year repayment term beginning in 2015 and a final maturity of June 2022. Assets purchased using the amounts borrowed under Nextel Brazil's equipment financing facilities are pledged as collateral.According to the US Securities and Exchange Commission, Section 10.4 of the report indicates that Nextel Brazil amended and restated both the Sinosure and non-Sinosure credit agreements it made with China Development Bank in 2012. 'Final Amendments' include 1) the deferral of principal payments and certain financial compliance for the first 48 months until June 30 2020, 2) Nextel Brazil will be subject to minimum cash and minimum receivable requirements, 3) Loan maturity will be 98 months from when Final Amendments become effective. Additionally, Nextel Brazil will grant additional security interests to CDB and Banco de Brasil SA and Caiza Economica Federal (the latter two have separate agreements with Nextel) in the form of preferential rights to amounts held in Nextel Brazil’s bank accounts and will pledge certain of its equipment and property to these lenders.

Title: XCMG Construction Loans 24,200 USD to Exito Import/Export S.A.
Description: In June 2012, XCMG Construction loaned 24,200 USD to Exito Import/Export S.A. as import financing. The loan terms and use of the loan are unclear. The Central Bank of Brazil lists the registration of the loan as complete.

Title: China Development Bank loans $48,800,000 to State Grid Brazil Holding S.A.
Description: In September 2012, China Development Bank provided a $48,800,000 loan to State Grid Brazil Holding S.A. for its subsidiary Serra Paracatu Transmissora de Energia S.A. (SPTE). According to page 6 of the report released by State Grid Corporation of China, the loan has an interest rate of LIBOR + 2.85% (Sep. 2012 6 month average LIBOR + 2.85=3.522%). The period of maturity is 12 years with payments of principal and semi-annual interest.In 2014, State Grid International Development Co. Ltd (SGID), as guarantor, settled the entire outstanding balance in one total amount of $43,260,012 on behalf of the company. By way of background, in December 2010, State Grid Corporation of China acquired 100% stakes in seven Brazilian transmission concession companies through its wholly owned Brazilian subsidiary, State Grid Brazil Holding S.A. (“SGBH”), each with a secured 30-year operating right. The assets in these companies at the time of acquisition included transmission lines with a total length of 3,176 km, six 500 kV substations, two 500 kV switching stations and one 345 kV substation. SGBH assumed responsibility for operating these 7 transmission companies. Then, in May 2012, SGBH entered into an agreement to acquire another seven Brazilian transmission concession companies, each with a secured 30-year operating right. The seven companies’ assets at the time of acquisition included transmission lines with a total length of 2,792 km, 11 self-owned substations and 12 substations jointly controlled with others. These activities and other electricity transmission projects — including the 2,518 km Belo Monte ±800 kV UHVDC Transmission Project (or 巴西美丽山水电特高压直流送出二期项 or ±800千伏特高压直流输电工程) — have been supported by China Development through a series of loans.

Title: Agricultural Bank of China loans $25,000,000 to Cimento Tupi S.A.
Description: On January 8 2013, the Agricultural Bank of China loaned $25,000,000 USD to Cimento Tupi S.A., a Brazilian cement and concrete products company. The Brazil Central Bank lists the loan registration as complete. According to the Cimento Tupi S.A. Financial Statements for December 2013 (pgs 35, 36, 51), the interest rate is 12-month Libor (0.683%) + 2.80% = 3.483%. The principal is to be paid semi-annually from August 20, 2014 and the interest will be paid semi-annually from February 20, 2013. The final maturity is February 20, 2017 (four years). The same document implies that the transaction is connected to a loan agreement for the expansion of the Pedra do Sino plant.As of a 2017 financial report published by Cimento Tupi S.A., the company had missed principal and interest payments due August 2015, February and August 2016, and February 2017.

Description: In December 2014, the Bank of China loaned $6,800,000 to Vale Brazil, a Brazilian multinational corporation engaged in metals and mining. The loan terms and the use of the loan is unclear. The Brazil Central Bank lists the registration as complete, but the status of the loan is unclear.

Title: CDB provides $1.5 billion loan tranche to Petrobras for oil exploration and extraction activities (Linked to Project ID#38170)
Description: On April 1, 2015, Petroleo Brasileiro S.A. (Petrobras) signed an agreement with China Development Bank (CDB) for a loan (with two tranches) worth $5 billion that would support oil exploration and extraction activities. The loan carried a 12-year maturity (final maturity date: 2027). Its interest rate is unknown. The loan was not collateralized. However, as a condition of the loan agreement, Petrobras assumed a preferential supply obligation of 100,000 barrels/day to three Chinese companies [China National United Oil Corporation (a subsidiary of CNPC), China Zhenhua Oil Co. Ltd., and Chemchina Petrochemical Co. Ltd (a subsidiary of ChemChina)]. The first, $3.5 billion tranche of the loan was fully disbursed in April 2015 (as captured via Project ID#38170). The second, $1.5 billion tranche of the loan was authorized by CDB on May 19, 2015 and fully disbursed on June 12, 2015 (as captured via Project ID#52918). On December 16, 2019, Petrobras made full prepayment of the $5 billion CDB loan, effectively terminating its obligation to supply a total volume of 100,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day to Chinese companies on a preferential basis. This loan agreement was a source of controversy in Brazil. Leaked information — published by a Brazilian newspaper — revealed that the 60% of the CDB loan proceeds were to be used to buy Chinese equipment. The ‘local content’ clause in the loan agreement also provoked disagreements between Petrobras and ANP, the government entity that oversees Brazil’s oil sector. Complying with a request from the Securities and Exchange Commission of Brazil, Petrobras made a public statement about the issue. It clarified that, due to previous agreements, the local content condition was already fulfilled so there was no need for it to acquire additional Chinese equipment.

Title: China Development Bank provides $600 million preferential buyer's credit facility with Telemar for the purchase of equipment and services from Huawei (linked to #59611)
Description: In December 2015, the China Development Bank entered into two $600 million preferential buyer's credit facility agreements with Telemar Norte Leste S.A. (“TMAR”), a wholly owned subsidiary of Oi S.A. The purpose of one of the agreements is the refinancing of its debt and the debt of its parent company Oi and the other is the financing the purchase of equipment and services from Huawei Technologies.According to the Oi 2015 Management report, 632.5 million USD were drawn from the 2 lines of credit, of which US$600 million mature in 2020 and US$32.5 million mature in 2025.The portion of the facility financing the purchase of equipment and services from Huawei Technologies has a 9.5 year maturity, no grace period, and an interest rate of 6 month Libor + 2% margin (December 2015 Avg. 6 month Libor + 2% = 2.764%).This project is linked to project #59611 which records the other 600 million credit facility.Later that year, Oi filed for bankruptcy, at which both sides agreed upon delaying payments on the company's CDB debt until 2023 at the earliest. It is estimated Oi owed the CDB $650 million.

Title: China Development Bank loans $75,600,000 to BBCA Brazil for Mato Grosso do Sul Facility
Description: In 2013, according to Macauhub, Chinese group BBCA signed a letter of intent to invest 320 million USD in the agricultural sector of Mato Grosso do Sul in order to increase food production and the volume of state exports to China. According to page 7 of the BRICS working paper by Gustavo Oliveira, BBCA is building a maize/soybean processing and citric acid production factory ('indústria de processamento de milho e soja'). It is set to be in Maracaju, in the state of Mato Grosso do Sul. The project would be a partnership between BBCA and the city of Maracaju in the state of Mato Grosso do Sul. On May 19 2015, according to the state government of Mato Grosso do Sul, governor Reinaldo Azambuja signed the cooperation agreement between the state government, BBCA, and CDB for the processing plant. The plant has an estimated processing capacity of 1.25 million tons/year of corn and 1 million tons/year of soy. The plant will also process corn products: 150,000 tons of citric acid, 60,000 tons of maltose syrup, 300,000 tons of cornstarch, 60,000 tons of crystalline dextrose, 150,000 tons of lysine and others; and soybean - 170 thousand tons of soybean oil and 810 thousand tons of bean pulp.In September 2015, the China Development Bank loaned $75,600,000 to BBCA Brazil Industrial and Investments LTDA ('BBCA BRAZIL INDUSTRIAL E INVESTIMENTOS LTDA'), the Brazilian branch of the agricultural and science-tech industry company China BBCA Group Co. Ltd. The status and loan terms are unknown. The Brazil Central Bank lists the loan registration as complete. Although the Central Bank source doesn't explicitly link the loan to the project, it is likely since this project is one of the few within the right 2015 timeframe that associates BBCA and CDB in Brazil.In 2016, according to the state government of Mato Grosso do Sul, there were delays in the progress of the facility and a Chinese delegation was sent to affirm Chinese commitment to acceleration project implementation. An update from the state government in 2018 maintained that there were still delays in the project due to late delivery of works and product imports, and budget issues. What has been completed as of January 2018 includes support infrastructure for the main project: 2 warehouses, a shed, and 30 houses for the Chinese workers who will be working on the processing facility. The new estimated completion date is the end of 2019. An update in December 2018 by the state government cited more delays due to strong winds in the region but clarified a new completion date for October 2020 to start the production of cornstarch and citric acid.

Title: CDB loan to 600 MW Pedras Altas power station Units 1 and 2 (linked to #59266)
Description: China Development Bank made a 800 million USD loan pledge in 2015 for the 600 MW Ouro Negro Coal-Fired Power Plant Project [or the Pedras Altas power station power station Units 1 and 2 (300MW each)]. Pedras Altas power station, known locally as UTE Ouro Negro, is a proposed 600-megawatt (MW) coal-fired power plant in Pedras Altas, Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil. The total cost of the project is approximately $1 billion and CDB pledged to cover 80% of the cost. This project is supposed to be implemented by Ouro Negro Energia in partnership with SEPCO2 and its subcontractor (Northwest Electric Power Design Institute). An EPC contract was signed in the fall of 2019.

Title: China Construction Bank loans R$ 96,894,000 for the Belo Monte Transmissora de Energia S.A  [linked to #59863 and #59898]
Description: In 2016, the China Construction Bank loaned 93,100,000 Brazilian Reals to Eletronorte (an electricity a generation and transmission subsidiary of Eletrobras) for the Belo Monte Transmissora de Energia S.A., which is a special purpose vehicle created to construct transmissions lines from the Belo Monte dam on the Xingu River in the state of Para, to the Brazilian Southeast. According to the SEC, the disbursed amount was 96,894,000 Brazilian Reals. The transaction amount has been recorded as such. The transmission line will have 2,100 kilometers and will cross four states: Pará, Tocantins, Goiás, and Minas Gerais. It begins at the Xingu Substation, located approximately 17 kilometers from the Belo Monte Hydropower Dam, in the municipality of Anapu in Para Sate, and extends to the Estreito Substation, located in the municipality of Ibiraci, in Minas Gerais, close to the border with São Paulo State. The estimated construction cost is R $5.1 billion (US $1.6 billion) and the projected start of operations is early 2018. State Grid won the bid for the construction of the first transmission line in February 2014, forming a consortium called Belo Monte Transmissão de Energia (BMTE). State Grid retained control with 51 percent, whereas two minority partners are controlled by Eletrobras: Eletronorte (24.5 percent) and Furnas (24.4 percent). The converting stations have completed the earthworks, and civil works are already underway. The assembly of structures is in progress, according to the Agência Nacional de Energia Elétrica (ANEEL) and the agency responsible for evaluating the safety and continuity of Brazil’s electricity supply. As of April 2017, the degree of fulfillment of the contract was 67 percent for physical development and 87 percent for general development. However, problems were reported in some parts of the work. According to Costa (2017), the Chinese contractor SEPCO1, responsible for sections 1 and 2 of the project, had reached 20 percent and 57 percent of the planned work in early 2017, respectively. In the other 6 sections, the project was nearly 78% complete. According to the World Bank PPI, State Grid Corporation of China provided 316.8 million USD worth of equity, giving it 51% ownership of the project.

Title: Bank of China loans R$ 6,310,000 for the Belo Monte Transmissora de Energia S.A [#59898 and #59891]
Description: In 2016, the Bank of China loaned for 6,125,000 Brazilian Reals to Eletronorte (an electricity a generation and transmission subsidiary of Eletrobras) for the Belo Monte Transmissora de Energia S.A., which is a special purpose vehicle created to construct transmissions lines from the Belo Monte dam on the Xingu River in the state of Para. As of 2018, the project was nearing completion and operation.According to the World Bank PPI, State Grid Corporation of China provided 316.8 million USD worth of equity, giving it 51% ownership of the project.

Title: China Exim Bank loans $100,170,884.17 to CMOC Brasil
Description: In October 2016, China Exim Bank loaned $100,170,884.17 USD to the CMOC Brasil Servicos Administrativos e Participacoes, a subsidiary of China Molybdenum Company Ltd (CMOC) that specializes in resource extraction.The status and loan terms are unclear. According to the Brazil Central Bank, the registration of the loan is complete.

Title: CDB provides first, $5 billion loan tranche to Petrobras to help it meet its repayment obligations (Linked to Project ID#53067 and ID#59384)
Description: On February 26, 2016, Petroleo Brasileiro S.A. (Petrobras) and China Development Bank (CDB) signed a term sheet for a $10 billion line of credit (captured via Project ID#53067). The line of credit was to be disbursed in two, $5 billion tranches (with 10 year maturities) and used by the borrower to address its maturing debt obligations. Petrobras and CDB signed a $5 billion agreement for the first loan tranche on December 15, 2016 (captured via Project ID#53068). This loan tranche disbursed on December 22, 2016. Then, on December 14, 2017,  Petrobras and CDB signed a $5 billion agreement for the second loan tranche (captured via Project ID#59384), which fully disbursed in December 2017 and January 2018. As a source of collateral for the first loan tranche, Petrobras signed oil-supply contracts with three Chinese companies [China National United Oil Corporation (a subsidiary of CNPC), China Zhenhua Oil Co. Ltd. (a subsidiary of NORINCO), and Chemchina Petrochemical Co. Ltd (a subsidiary of ChemChina)] on December 15, 2016. Under each contract, Petrobras agreed to sell 100,000 barrels of oil per day for 10 years at market prices. As a source of collateral for the second loan tranche, Petrobras signed an oil-supply contract with UNIPEC Asia — Sinopec’s (subsidiary) trading company — and agreed to provide 100,000 barrels per day for 10 years at market prices. Petrobras reportedly repaid a small portion of the $10 billion debt between 2016 and 2018. However, by the end of 2019, it had repaid $8 billion (including a $5 billion repayment on December 16, 2019).

Title: China Exim Bank loans $46,151,338.05 USD to CMOC Brasil in October 2016
Description: In October 2016, the China Exim Bank loaned $46,151,338.05 to CMOC Brasil Servicos Administrativos e Participacoes, a subsidiary of China Molybdenum Company Ltd (CMOC) that specializes in resource extraction.The status and loan terms are unclear. According to the Brazil Central Bank, the registration of the loan is complete.

Title: China Eximbank provides a $900 million USD disbursement to Petrobras (Linked to Project ID#59380)
Description: In May 2016, the Export-Import Bank of China signed a term agreement for a loan of $1 billion USD to Petrobras via a line of credit to Petrobras Global Trading BV. The agreement provided funds for equipment and goods that Petrobras has already contracted with other Chinese firms. Petrobras intended to use the funds to relieve outstanding debt to these other firms and forward its 2018-2022 Business Plan and Management strategy to diversify funding sources. On May 30, 2018, the first disbursement of $900 million USD was provided to Petrobras through the line of credit. The remaining balance of $100 million USD was expected to be disbursed by May 2019.Petrobras is insured by Sinosure, China's export credit agency. According to the National People's Congress of China, this project represents part of the China EXIM Bank and Petrobras' May 2015 'Memorandum of Funding on Supporting China-Brazil Offshore Engineering Equipment Capacity Cooperation', which stipulates that China EXIM Bank will provide credit support for Petrobras oil to purchase offshore engineering equipment in exchange for Petrobras exporting petroleum products to China.

Title: China Development Bank loans $20,000,000 to XCMG Brasil
Description: In June 2017, China Development Bank loaned $20,000,000 to XCMG Brasil Industria Ltda ('XCMG BRASIL IND.LTDA.'), the Brazilian branch of the Chinese construction equipment supplying company XCMG Construction. The loan status, terms and purpose are unclear. The Brazil Central Bank lists the loan registration as complete.

Title: China Construction Bank Refinances $166.67 Million of Petrobras Debt (Linked to #59388)
Description: On November 16, 2017, China Construction Bank signed a financing agreement for $166.67 million loan of debt financing to Petrobras. The loan is part of a $1 billion syndicated loan for the refinancing of Petrobras' debt, headed by Standard Chartered Bank. The other portion of Chinese financing is $166.67 million from the ICBC (#59388). The loan details are unclear. According to Petrobras, the loan will be guaranteed by Petrobras’s P-56 platform.

Title: CDB Loans $13,000,000 to XCMG Brazil in December 2017

The loan status, terms and purpose are unclear. The Brazil Central Bank lists the loan registration as complete.

Title: CDB Loans $5,000,000 to XCMG Brazil in December 2017
Description: In December 2017, the China Development Bank loaned $5,000,000 to XCMG Brasil. The loan terms and use of the loan are unclear. The Central Bank of Brazil lists the registration of the loan as completed, however the current status is unclear.

Title: China Exim Bank loans $300 million to Banco do Brasil for Trade Financing
Description: On September 1 2017, China Exim bank signed a major financing terms agreement for the loan of $300 million to Banco do Brasil as working capital for China-Brazil trade financing. The move will improve the liquidity of Banco do Brasil. The signing was witnessed by Chinese President Xi Jinping and Former Brazil President Michel Temer.The loan terms are unclear.
